replies.author: @RegularCupOfJoe
replies.text: I hope people who read C.S. Lewis quotes can also come to understand who he was.  He is often overshadowed by Tolkien because of the success of The Hobbit and the Lord of the Rings trilogy.  But Lewis was the more experienced of the two, and more inquisitive.  Lewis also had a wider range of interests compared to Tolkien.  He was an atheist that would put modern atheists to shame.  He then converted to Christianity "kicking and screaming" (his words) and became a prolific writer of Christian stories.  Yet he still continued his insightful commentary on many things.  Having fought in World War 1 and lived through World War 2 and then into the 60s, he saw the full force of post-modern deconstructionism take hold of the world.  The first victim of post-modern deconstruction was the ideal (and idea) of good and evil.  World War 1 posited the idea that the boundaries of good and evil aren't always so clear, and then World War 2 enshrined it.  Then the social movements of the 50s and 60s made it malignant.  C.S. Lewis knew this better than anyone and this is why his writings are still important.  He is spot on that we need stories of brave knights.

text: “It's like in the great stories, Mr. Frodo. The ones that really mattered. Full of darkness and danger they were. And sometimes you didn't want to know the end. Because how could the end be happy? How could the world go back to the way it was when so much bad had happened? But in the end, it’s only a passing thing, this shadow. Even darkness must pass. A new day will come. And when the sun shines it will shine out the clearer. Those were the stories that stayed with you. That meant something, even if you were too small to understand why. But I think, Mr. Frodo, I do understand. I know now. Folk in those stories had lots of chances of turning back, only they didn’t. They kept going, because they were holding on to something. That there is some good in this world, and it's worth fighting for.”

author: @chrishamm2515
text: I saw an article some years ago saying that once upon a time C.S Lewis was talking with Tolkien, as they frequently did for many years, discussing the nature of escapism. The article claimed that Lewis had recounted the story saying that Tolkien asked him, "Who could possibly be against escapism," To which Lewis replied that he did not know. Tolkien's response was a single word, "jailers." I have decided to believe this exchange happened, because even if it did not, that is the truth of the matter and fantasy, along with most of our culture for a decade or more has been in the hands of jailers determined to prevent even the slightest amount of escape. Their time must come to an end.

replies.text:
Here’s Tolkien expanding this idea in his essay “on fairy stories”:

Why should a man be scorned, if, finding himself in prison, he tries to get out and go home? Or if, when he cannot do so, he thinks and talks about other topics than jailers and prison-walls? The world outside has not become less real because the prisoner cannot see it. In using Escape in this way the critics have chosen the wrong word, and, what is more, they are confusing, not always by sincere error, the Escape of the Prisoner with the Flight of the Deserter. just so a Party-spokesman might have labeled departure from the misery of the Fuhrer's or any other Reich and even criticism of it as treachery .... Not only do they confound the escape of the prisoner with the flight of the deserter; but they would seem to prefer the acquiescence of the "quisling" to the resistance of the patriot.

replies.author: @collinmassie4966
replies.text: Believe it or not, the scene did happen. Tolkien says something to that effect in his 'Essay on Fairy Stories' that C. S. Lewis worked with him to publish, referencing it in his later 'Experiment in Literary Criticism.' But the specific scene happened before the essay was published and Lewis mentions it in a letter to Arthur C. Clarke in 1954 without specifying Tolkien's name: "About 'escapism', never let that flea stick in your ear. I was liberated from it once and for all when a friend [Tolkien] said 'These critics are very sensitive to the least hint of Escape. Now what class of men wd. one expect to be thus worked up about Escape?--Jailers.' Turn-key critics: people who want to keep the world in some ideological prison because a glimpse at any remote prospect wd. make their stuff seem less exclusively important."

@f@frankvandorp9732ose two things are usually the opposite.

This isn’t to say that a story taking place in the real world is realism, just that the plot events/characters/etc, evolve in realistic portrayals.

Escapism is inherently much more fantastical, and separate from the real world and realistic portrayal of characters, plot etc. And moral complexity is itself a thing you see in realism, where moral simplicity is much more tied to escapism.

You have moral complexity in escapism, it just takes it a bit further away from being perfect escapism. And like I said before, we all have different immersion thresholds for different levels of realism in a story

replies.author: @KimballKinnison01
replies.text:
​@PastorPat23Well I do have critical faculties. You are supporting a statement with a text that is not grounded in truth and which, as a sacred text, also helped shape the world view of the person making the original statement. The logic is circular and the sentiment not based on the human condition. Maybe it's aspirational, but for a society long since gone. 

"Evil" is a relative term, at best. But what we call evil is not usually the ugly, monstrous thing that Tolkien makes his antagonists. Evil is attractive or evil would have no power. Simplifying a struggle to beautiful Vs ugly, to me, serves no purpose. That's not life. Real monsters star on reality shows and then run for public office. People love the monsters or the monsters would have no power.

For me, the best fantasy is grounded in moral relativism, in a world view I can accept. I don't want Prince Valiant. Conan isn't Good, he's good at what he does. Waylander is driven to do good out of self-interest. John Wick kills hundred to avenge a murdered puppy.

author: @TheEnderBand
text:
It's almost like age-old archetypes exist for a reason; they work and people can relate to them


EDIT: because the comment section has turned into a ridiculous quagmire (giggity) of people making dumb strawman arguments, allow me to clarify: 

I'm not saying that everything need be archetype and I'm not saying that the only good stories are cookiecutter ones. I simply acknowledge that archetypes work and always have  

In fact, perhaps to your surprise, my personal favorite stories and characters are complex. I am absolutely capable of enjoying storytelling with nuance and actually prefer it believe it or not- WHEN and IF it's done well. And that's a big "when" and a big "if". 

A perfect example of this is the first season of True Detective- it has exactly my favorite type of protagonist: complex, flawed, even anti-heroic at times, but ultimately through his experience and action is an heroic character- just not in a storybook sense.

I recognize the power and efficacy of archetypes/tropes. And I think that much of the bad storytelling done over the last couple of decades (or longer for that matter) has been largely due to the total abandonment of these conventions- People get on a postmodernist/deconstructionist kick and end up throwing the baby out with the bathwater and trying to reinvent the wheel every time, often to the detriment of the story.

If this were a cooking channel and Drinker was making some Ribeye on the grill and I said "It's almost like salt and charcoal grilling exists for a reason- they taste good and people like them" and then every comment was someone going
 "MAYBE YOU JUST AREN'T CAPABLE OF TASTING THE OTHER 4 PRIMARY FLAVORS," or "SOUS VIDE IS DELICIOUS YOU ARE JUST NOT SOPHISTICATED ENOUGH TO APPRECIATE THE SUBTLETY,"  I'd think those people were missing the point and even perhaps, just a wee bit stupid, wouldn't you? If not- you might just be one of those wee bit stupid people and that's ok- *somebody* has the be on the outside of the Bell Curve, and it may as well be the Bell Ends

You can advocate for something without automatically denouncing everything else not explictly mentioned in the endorsement. My initial comment was not me saying "Oh this is the only type of story that should exist and the only kind I can understand" but I suppose, ironically, some people are incapable of not seeing the world in binary black and white

Ah, irony- like goldy and bronzey but made of iron

replies.author: @doorman5966
replies.text: It's biologically driven. In the West, it  has to do with Aryan expectations on behavior. That is why the most famous ancient work is Beowulf (about a warrior who defends a village from a foreign threat) or the Odyssey (about a warrior king who returns home to save his kingdom from usurpers).  Occasionally Hollywood did embrace it--like in the 1930s when they did Errol Flynn movies to support the war effort (the 1938 Robin Hood is rather fascistic by today's standards). Hollywood is much more fond of the Aladdin or David archtype--and it is oriental (not including Japan, which has the warrior tradition too). David is the wimpy youth who defeats the giant with a slingshot throw. In traditional knight adventures, the warrior fights in hand to hand combat-not with projectile weapons.  Luke Skywalker is a David analog, so is Martin Brody in Jaws, they both defeat Goliath with a projectile weapon. technological crutches.  And if it isn't David, then it is Samson-who usually has a self-destructive element.  In Hollywood, big strong guys are either depicted as morons or bullies (Biff Tannen being a prime example of this).

replies.author: @L1623VP
replies.text: Yes, if you're familiar with professor Joseph Campbell and his work in comparative mythology, then you know his The Hero's Journey sequence of events is reflected in all the greatest stories since storytelling began. Those are the stories that last through the ages. They're also the stories that resonate with us because The Hero's Journey reflects our own struggles through the "dark night of the soul" and our desire to conquer them. If any story is missing The Hero's Journey or any part of it, it feels unsatisfying to us, repulsive, or forgettable. The Hero's Journey is really our journey, the one we're all taking in our lives, and that's what we instinctively desire to see reflected in the stories we read, watch, and share.

replies.author: @doorman5966
replies.text: @L1623VP Joseph Campbell simplified stories to fit his theories. Some of his steps don't apply to every character like "refusal to the call" or 'counsel from a mentor.' Zorro didn't refuse the call. And neither did Flash Gordon or Prince Valiant or Conan. Odysseus was mentored by a deity--that's a personification  of Nature and personality not a mentor. At its basic level, Beowulf is about the strongest member of the tribe being called on to protect the tribe. Odysseus was a king who needs to return home to reclaim his kingdom. These ideas would be considered fascist now--but in older times, they were considered the normal part of tribal existence. The Song of Roland ends with a horrible act of torture and mass execution! Flash Gordon and Prince Valiant were very popular comic strips because the concept of the brave adventurer appealed to most people of Aryan ancestry (and some others).  Despite the fame of Sinbad the Sailor, in the Arabian Peninsula, the medieval warrior poet Antar was considered more favourably--and who in the West has heard of him?

replies.author: @Adamguy2003
replies.text:
Yes!
In most modern fantasy and sci- fi, it's written by people who unwittingly subscribe to what I like to call "The Voldemort Philosophy";
In 'Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone,' Lord Voldemort at one point infamously says "There is no good or evil; There is only power, and those too weak to possess it."
Anyone who is able to think critically realizes that only a genuinely evil person would subscribe to such a philosophy, as a way to assuage what little remains of their consciences to  justify the hideous things they do.
Modern writers of fantasy and sci- fi, however, take similar philosophies as gospel truth and try to advance it through their cynical, mean- spirited works. It's why one of the major red flags behind the twisted morality of 'Star Wars: The Acolyte' for me was a scene when Lesbian Sith Witch Mama, a character meant to be seen as kind, wise and virtuous, has a line that is nearly identical to Voldemort's line.

text: People are drawn to tales that celebrate such virtues in its protagonists as heroism, sacrifice, bravery, perseverance, friendship, honesty and integrity because they stir something in us. We recognise these values to be true and valuable and something to be strived for and it wells up a desire to pursue them in ourselves. There is room for such themes as revenge, tragedy and irony but the stories we were drawn to as kids and the ones that will have the greatest emotional impact on us will always be those of sincerity.

Oda has reason to be concerned about it. He's still making his series, unlike many other IPs which are like 20-30 years old at least, and made their money back then, HE'S still actively making his magnum opus.

Now, while a terrible Netflix anime adaptation wouldn't exactly be newsworthy, I can also see him wanting to protect his baby nonetheless.

Plus, as a big Dragonball fan himself, I am certain that Oda is aware of how badly that was butchered in live action (Not by Netflix, but still).

I am convinced the only reason it is the best anime adaptation in live action put out by Netflix is because of how involved Oda was, because even with season 1, I could see the signs of where Netflix would have butchered it given the chance.

Survivorship bias applies when looking back at something and you (incorrectly) judge it's quality based only on the successes and ignore the failures, but this is about comparing two different sides and which one has successfully done more of something when looking back at both.
It's not survivorship bias when the question itself is "how many survivors are there on either side?"

replies.author: @kerim.peardon5551
replies.text:
I'm writing an urban fantasy series right now myself. It was anime-inspired and is in the slice-of-life style and meant to be very long-running. It's not like anything we have in the West, but I'm just writing it for me, because I enjoy it. The story follows a family, and it tends to focus more on the men in the family (including ones who marry into it). The family undergoes a series of unfortunate events, where each person suffers their own trial and tribulations, but they're always concerned with doing what's right and they always double down on family. They have their various flaws, but they work on them, with the help of their family, and grow into better people. The original MC goes from a failure-to-launch college dropout at 19 to a married man with 5 kids and three grandchildren and he's a highly-decorated fire chief by the time he's 50. He's also got a couple of kills under his belt because he doesn't play when people try to attack his family.

I don't have any kids to pass my stories down to, but I enjoy reading them for myself nonetheless. It's nice to read about genuinely good people trying to make their way in the world and trying to make the world a better place. Also, they're normal. No 7' men with shoulders a yard wide and 12 pack abs, dressed in leather and lurking in their own private BDSM dungeon. My people are normally-proportioned, and the most abnormal thing they do is live an off-grid prepper lifestyle in rural Idaho (where they're not considered odd at all living like that). So much of paranormal romance and even straight-up fantasy these days is ridiculously over-the-top when it comes to what people look like, especially men.

@kerim.peardon5551
replies.text:
@MaryRohwer LOL. I hadn't thought about that, but it is rural fantasy! 

"Urban fantasy" is used, as far as I'm aware, for fantasy that overlaps the real world--regardless of whether it's actually in a city or not. In my story's case, everything exactly parallels the real world (minus Covid being a thing because that makes for a boring story for a couple of years), only vampires are real and they can do some stuff that defies the known laws of physics. I consider it an alternate timeline/parallel reality. 

Most people I read have their characters live in an almost fantasy-like world that is kind of behind the scenes or underground--kind of like Gaiman's Neverwhere, where there are people living an unseen, parallel existence just out of sight of the normal world--and they only briefly come out to interact with the real world, then they go back into the fantasy realm. 

My characters just live in the normal world; there's no hidden dimension to it. The original MC got the ability to control fire when he was turned into a vampire, and his wife said "become a firefighter," so that's what he did. One of his kids becomes a paramedic, two become cops, and one becomes a 911 operator/sheriff's dispatcher--all of them using their abilities as half-vampires to do an excellent job saving people's lives. (Only one of his kids is not a first responder, but she marries her brother's paramedic partner.) They catch all kinds of hell from the old guard, medieval-era vampires, though. That's always the largest source of their drama.

replies.text:
@RitzOriginal1 Yep. I'm that older guy (51) who pined for the way life was when he was growing up in the 80's and 90's. I fully understand that it is not the 80's and the 90's anymore. This is not an "old guy with a broken spirit and broken body and lots of regrets" situation. Quite the opposite, really. I pined for common decency and goodness. I pined to see and help others succeed. I pined for family and community. I pined for positivity and for being wrapped up in the community that resides in my immediate surroundings.

Well, I found it. All of it. Turns out it's all around us in the real world. The sooner you stop having someone else's version of reality fed to you through a screen, and spend that time in your own family and community, interacting with your friends and neighbors in person, you will also find it. You will discover the freedom of concerning yourself with people and places that you are familiar with.

Get away from the screens, social media, and television. Stop being fed all of the doom and gloom that concerns people on the other side of the earth, on the other side of the country, on the other side of the state, even on the other side of the city. Stop worrying about what the government's elites are saying and doing. Stop pretending that you are "staying informed". You have zero control over what they say and do, and simply consuming media is not political activism. It's there that you will find peace and freedom and finally cleanse your soul of the toxicity that you are willingly consuming day after day.

If you choose otherwise, you have no one to blame but yourself if you and the world immediately around you are miserable. It takes zero effort to do nothing and consume media. If you pine for a more masculine world, but choose the comforts of doomscrolling, you are the problem, and are making a conscious choice.

So yeah…the nice guy would be considered virtuous. If he’s punching spoiled prince’s teeth loose knowing he would get quartered by horses for it then he is impulsive, But mainly brave and doing the right thing despite the danger. It shows he isn’t the wittiest as a wiser man would try to talk his way out of the violence, but he is heroic and led by his emotions and temper. he raced in because of the girl he liked and the fact that what the prince was doing was wrong. 

It’s just that dunk is graceless and less charismatic as the typical archetype. (I think he’s charismatic. But it’s clearly different. He’s charming for his heart, but I just mean socially he stumbles.) 

He doesn’t really complain, either. He can get despondent and doubt, but he still pushes through and does what he says he wants to do despite all that. So he’s brave and not a whinging c*nt.

The gentle giant trope is loved by women. He probably doesn’t even realize him asking the girl to paint his shield endeared her. He regards her talent and wasn’t a simp about it. (He was flustered and shy, but he wasn’t there wagging his tongue at her like a dog hoping her service to him was some prelude to sex.)

Considering his character has pages of history on him for his actions means he’s not a fence sitter and he just did the damn thing. So we need more time with him.

replies.text: I'm happy to see Frieren getting mostly positive comments in Drinker's audience. The show is so damn good, but really quite unusual in its tone and pacing. It's so self-reflective, and zen, and really has cool insights on the intersection of remorse and personal growth, but in a way that is warm and mostly optimistic rather than negative/cynical.  It's also a show that proves (yet again) that you can make fantasy that is not just testosterone-overdrive BASH BASH, stack it with multiplecentral female characters, and still have a show that is great fantasy for everyone to love while ALSO being deeply insightful and truthful about how the genders interact. Highly recommended.

replies.text:
​@jeddpires  it does subvert expectations, but not in the way you think. Himmel is a hero unworthy of the world's version of excalibur, Heither/Sein are "corrupt priests", Eisen/Stark are "cowardly warriors", and Frieren is an elf that isn't a fountain of wisdom and is still learning. There's other subversions of tropes from other characters and tropes I'm too busy to name. But the subversion of expectations aren't there to break ankles with a crossover and leave us confused and shocked, but to build characters up and make them MORE.

Himmel is unworthy of Excalibur? Ah, well, he'll be the hero anyway. He doesn't need a sword to be the "Hero". He's the "Hero" because he's Himmel. 

Eisen and Stark are on the verge of pissing themselves? The only time one is brave is when they're afraid. Watch them stand their ground anyway and prove their quality.

See how they subvert the fantasy tropes assigned to them? Ain't that beautiful?

​@marcpeycker I 100% get what you mean. The bar is in hell lol, but to offer a caveat to the commenter you replied to, I've seen many shows, even ones that rose to S-Tier status (at least for me) that had kinda rough starts. The Expanse was one of them. I know this doesn't really count, but Dragonball Z Abridged is another. FMA Brotherhood has a really hectic start. But all of those rose to be among my favorite shows.

The new LA Airbender is based on what is already one my highest S-Tier shows (that I dont think can be topped with live actors), but it could still significantly improve after its first season.

Here's a thought (not snarky, I mean this genuinely): how many shows have you seen that had a stellar first season... and kept getting better and finished strong? On the inverse, how many shows have you seen start mid, just barely above average, but then become phenomenal by the end? For my sake, I've been let down by more shows that start amazing (and then eventually shit the bed) than I have been impressed by shows that started mid.

replies.author: @BrandyGunderson
replies.text:
@Lil_Paulie”Because people with no honor are willing to do things to win/survive that people with honor will not.”

People with honor live in a world filled with people without honor.  And they know it.  It’s really not too difficult to identify which people are honorable and which are not.  Because people without honor constantly betray their utter lack of understanding of the concept in their daily interactions.

When you have the leaders of major factions succeeding in their machinations because they are willing to ignore the dictates of honor in pursuit of their goal, how long do you think they will remain the leaders of a major faction?  How long will they retain the loyalty of their subordinates, their foot soldiers, their pledged knights when those leaders betray their oaths to their followers All The Time?  Loyalty in the medieval world was reciprocal.  Failure to honor your obligations to your men quickly resulted in having no men.

So yes, the dishonorable are willing to do things to win that the honorable will not.  But the dishonorable will also refuse to do things to win that the honorable will.  Like keep an oath when doing so is disadvantageous in the moment.

Consider that in GoT the Lannister motto is “A Lannister always pays his debts.”  This does not, in fact, mean that a Lannister pays his debts.  And everyone knows it.  It means “A Lannister will always have his revenge.”  And everyone knows that, as well.  Because the only “debt” a Lannister will acknowledge is a debt owed to him (whether real or imagined), not a debt he owes another.   So who signs on to serve such a master?  No one honorable, that’s for sure.  And in a world bound by honor, being known as dishonorable isn’t a winning hand.

replies.author: @-MuhammadLuthfiSeviansyah
replies.text:
Despite how dark the setting is, the source material, A Song of Ice and Fire books are arguably still clinging to the heroic fantasy tropes. 

Jon and Daenerys are prophesied heroes born to unite the people against the true threat. 

Stannis while being a stern and uncompromising man is one of the fairest rulers a medieval kingdom could've asked for. 

Catelyn's mercy towards Jaime might have been one of the reasons of the Starks defeat but it set Jaime towards the path of redemption and he's doing all he can to uphold his oath.

Ned's honor even though it leads to his death is more rewarding in the long run rather than Tywin's cruel method of ruling, as the Stark's bannermen all secretly (even openly) conspire to kill off the Boltons and Freys and to restore house Stark as the defacto ruler of the North.

Meanwhile none of Tywin's bannermen actually respect him and once he's gone, the Lannister's grip is severely weaken. Should the Lannister be defeated, none of the other houses in the Westerland would have fought for a Lannister restoration. 

Dumb & Dumber decided that the dark setting and unexpected death are what the fans actually want so decided to take all of this away from the Game of Thrones series.

text: I know you're not a big anime guy, but I'm going to recommend an anime for you: I'm a Spider? It's about a class of high school kids who get teleported to an isekai world. All of the students become human heroes with various classes, but one of the characters gets transformed into a lowly spider, one of the weakest forms of life. As the show unfolds, the spider undergoes different battles and somehow manages to gain in level and strength and become the most powerful being in the fantasy world. Watching that unfold was really good storytelling, in my book. Sadly, the wheels really fall off the show at the end, so adjust your expectations. It might be something to check out just for if you're curious or if anyone reading this is curious.

author: @lulawentworth
text: Amen. For me, this is the Andor entry of Martin's universe. I'm blown away at the balance they've struck. Every detail feeds into the central theme of unmasking the costume of knighthood (and narrative), and the fact that a mere three episodes in I'm experiencing real human emotion for characters who behave like real human beings. Casting is immaculate, the storytellers understand/respect the story, and the demonstration that big spectacle doesn't necessarily mean better is a gift to fantasy. Seven hells it feels good to root for an uncomplicated hero again to instead ponder why it's hard to be a hero in a complicated and often corrupt world.

​@Oblivius33I think you misconstrued what I'm talking about. I said characters have to learn, grow and evolve, not necessarily become good.
As for evil winning in Moorcock, that depends on what character in the Eternal Champion Saga you're reading.
Moorcocks multiverse is based on a balance between order and chaos, not good vs evil. Sometimes evil wins and sometimes good does but to him that is secondary to balancing the scales of order and chaos. 
You're operating under the assumption that protagonist and hero are the same thing and they're not. Evil characters can grow and progress in entertaining ways just like their heroic counterparts.

replies.author: @Garthorium
replies.text:
​@liamjm9278 He's saying that to have the greatest understanding of these concepts you have to have some level of experiencing these things yourself. 

Tolkien understood the dynamics of brotherhood forged in battle because he himself served and attributes such characteristics to his own experiences as a sounding board.

How could I possibly write a good story about being a woman when I am not one? Sure, I can write one, but I'll never be able to translate the subtleties and nuances that come with being a woman appropriately because I am not one.

replies.author: @liamjm9278
replies.text:
@Garthorium So a good story is easier to write if you see your friends guts paint the side of a barnyard and are forced to charge into machine guns?

Sounds more like a fetishisation of suffering than a celebration or genuine insight into the creativity of man.

HG Wells never fought in a war, yet he revolutionised the sci-fi genre. Bram Stoker did the same to the Horror genre without need of trauma, as did Shelley at the age of 18 when she wrote Frankenstein. Conan Doyle never did any actual detective work. Gibson's knowledge of technology barely exceeded the Atari console when he wrote Neuromancer. Although Lord Byron died in the Greek War of Independence, his work was done before he personally took part in any war, and he didn't serve in any of the Coalitions against Napoleon; he even went out of his way to avoid Europe during it.

Suffering in things like war can give an inspiration of sorts, but it seems to me that the human experience is the true source of creativity, suffering being one aspect of it.

Millions of soldiers served, if war on its own made great writers, there'd be millions of great writers.

replies.text: ​@liamjm9278 No one said that serving is a requirement for good writers, this is a straw man. The OP was saying that experiencing darkness gives an appreciation of the light. "Write what you know" is still good advice, not in that we can only write about our experiences, but that the emotional resonance of the story must come from sincerity. Audiences can tell if a work is inauthentic or just a cash grab.

replies.author: @Garthorium
replies.text:
@liamjm9278  I think you're conflating my point entirely and you know exactly what I mean. 

I explicitly said to have the "greatest" understanding. There's a great difference between imagination and lived experience. Imagination is boundless but is only 'grounded' in its inspiration inasmuch as the person has had experience. 

You've got good points but I think you're being a little obtuse here. 

I could write a book about war through all I've experienced, but it holds little gravitas because as much as I am aware of the brotherhood that is formed in the military, and I comprehend its principles, I have never lived it, and thus I can literally only imagine what it is like, all the while being 'ignorant' of the true bond that is formed and how it affects the dynamism of the individual and the community it is involved in on an emotional, spiritual, and even physical level.

Does that make sense?

replies.author: @liamjm9278
replies.text:
@gray_mara Not explicitly, but they do act as it's some magic badge that automatically elevates a story over others.

I already acknowledged that it contributes, I just don't aggrandise it.

replies.author: @liamjm9278
replies.text:
​@Garthorium I gave examples of writers considered at the top of their genres who have little to no experience; so no, it isn't necessary to have the "greatest" understanding, but I do acknowledge that experience can elevate the quality in stories where there is relevant experience to draw from. But just because it *can,* it doesn't mean it *will.*

Brotherhood is an experience that is not monopolised by the horrors of war. It can be attained elsewhere, though the nuances and dynamics will be different. Military brotherhood is just one kind. An audience doesn't need to experience the military to know brotherhood, they can relate to it since they themselves may know a similar kind of brotherhood.

I believe that it is a gross fetishisation to believe that the greatest form of an aspect, such as "brotherhood" or other abstract qualities, can only be attained by the worst experiences and trials; It reminds me of the movie "Whiplash", and how the main villain is the personification of this fetishisation. I think an aspect used through the leans of a bad experience is just a different kind of the aspect, that will resonate in different ways that some may appreciate more than others. A different flavour of ice cream.

It is natural for people to hold an appreciation for the hardships, sacrifice and efforts to achieve a thing, so it would be natural for people to have an elevated level of appreciation and respect if it's revealed that the thing's achievement was done through a suffering of a sort. That elevated appreciation is artificial, for if the same thing was done without it, the appreciation would be less, even if the quality is the same. It's a self-appointed bias that has nothing to do with the quality of the work itself, but the method.

There are many works of mediocrity and terribleness that were made by those who have suffered. There are even writers who weaponise it against criticism or use it as promotion. Sometimes relieving yourself of a stubborn turd can be a hard task, but it's still shit.

I understand, and it has a merit of a sort, but not an absolute one. I cannot agree with it completely.

replies.text:
It's another of Martin's flaws though: What is the one thing common across all 3 of these series, besides the shared setting?

HE HASN'T FINISHED ANY OF THEM!

Granted, we "know" the final outcomes of both the Dance of Dragons, and of Duncan and Egg's adventures through wider lore notes, unlike with GoT . . . but the fact remains, he's started 3 different series in the same setting and he hasn't finished one of them.

I'm sorry Martin, you can't complain about the direction the showrunners are going in, if you yourself haven't even finished it for them to follow! Every time he's written it and they follow his guide it's amazing work, the moment they stray off heavily it sucks. But they wouldn't have the excuse to go off and do their own work, if his was done!

It's frustrating to watch, because yea Martin can write good material, and Knight of the Seven Kingdoms shows he can do a change of pace even within this setting and people can find it refreshing. But he's his own worst enemy.

replies.author: @NucleaRaptor
replies.text:
@JoeyM10 Here's what Song of Ice and Fire actually wants to be, and why George can't finish it. 


The Song of Ice and Fire isn't actually supposed to be dark, Machiavellian, hopeless, or a subversion of Tolkien at all. 

It's just supposed to start that way. 

The details may be complex, but the formula is simple. Low-fantasy version of the British Isles, torn apart by multi-sided Machiavellian power struggle, loosely based on the War of the Roses. 

Things are bad because of Machiavellian power struggle. 

In the background, subtle hints of external, magical, otherworldly threat. Warring factions scoff and ignore it as first. Enter the high-fantasy tropes; prophesied hero emerges to unite the morally-grey factions into an unambiguously-good pro-civilization force to confront and defeat the unambiguously-evil threat to all life. 

Full transition, in the end, to epic Tolkienesque high fantasy, played straight rather than subverted. 

Heroism triumphant, humanity triumphant, realm unified in peace and prosperity. 

Roll credits. 

Were the story to be completed thus, completed as it wants to be completed, as it yearns to be completed, every dark, gritty, Machiavellian moment would be fully justified. 

Every chapter and scene filled with thugs and villains and no heroes at all would be fully justified. 

Because they would merely serve to emphasize the rarity of heroes, and the need for them. 

Because they would make the arrival of a true hero that much more satisfying when, late but not too late, he arrived. 

ASOIAF doesn't really want to be a subversion of Tolkien at all. It wants to be a path out of darkness and into light. It wants to be a study in how Tolkien is deeply relevant, even to a gritty, morally grey world. 

This is what George knows it needs to be. 

But George cannot write it. 

Why? 

Because he's a socialist. And a boomer. 

Socialism's motivational core is envy, and its one underlying rule is "thou shalt not be better than me". 

The boomer's single guiding principle is "whatever makes me feel pleasure right now is good, and whatever makes me feel bad right now is evil". 

Take these together, and you get someone who has a real problem with heroes. Heroes are, by definition, the best of us, at least on some dimension, and if your underlying motivation is envy, standing next to one is gonna make you feel bad. 

This means that socialists, boomers, and socialist boomers tend not to want to believe in heroes and heroism. 

They want to convince themselves that anything which appears good is secretly evil, actually, and that anyone who makes them feel or look bad is obviously evil because reasons. 

So when they see a hero, they tend to call him a fascist. 

(Of course, when they see a fascist, they also call him a fascist, but that's just coincidence, because they'll call anything fascist... random passers-by, buildings, rocks, trees, squirrels, anything.)

Because they want to feel morally superior to him. 

The only way they can admit that someone has a moral compass at all is if they can feel superior to him in some other way, usually by portraying them as naive, and hence doomed to failure because he is not empowered by cynicism and selfishness, to pursue the most efficient path to... whatever. 

So if ol'George thinks that everyone who appears good is either secretly evil, or openly stupid, then writing a character with heroic impulses is gonna be tough, and writing about how they succeed... impossible. 

This is why George can write characters with noble motives (Jon Snow, Eddard Stark, etc), but he keeps making them fail. 

You see, in George's world, heroism must be a sham or a weakness, because then George's own bad  character is wisdom and enlightenment, instead of just lack of moral virtue. 

If heroes are all frauds or suckers, then George is being smart, because he has seen through the whole heroism thing. 

If heroes are real, and they do sometimes succeed, and they do make the world better for everyone, then George is just a fat, lazy, cynical old man who doesn't wanna finish his art for the sake of art or integrity, because he only ever wanted money, and now he has more than he knows what to do with. 

In order to finish the story, George would need to have an awakening of virtue. 

He would first have to develop a sense of integrity — a desire to fulfill his promises, even when no one can or will punish him for not doing so.

He would then have to develop a sense of humility — because to write a better person than he is, he would have to admit to himself that there is such a thing, that people can be better, and that trying to be better is an actual worthy goal, not just the act of falling for a con game run to control you. 

The longer someone goes without admitting to their faults, the harder those faults are to admit to, because they have been more deeply invested in. 

And this means he would also have to develop the courage to admit to himself that he is, in fact, a fat lazy cynical old coward, and that Tolkien, whom he envies and despises, was the far better man all along. (c) Devon Eriksen

Robert Jordans Wheel of Time series is great, but Amazon has probably killed it with their diversity hires making absolute woke garbage out of it. Raymond E Feist had lots of great books that would make great movies and even TV shows out of. David Eddings had some good books that weren't on the level of the earlier authors, but were still very entertaining and used somewhat different magic types. All of these are better than anything George RR Cantfinishastory writes.

author: @leiana_yt
text:
3:37 That's been one of the fundamental misunderstandings, "death came suddenly, without warning or 𝘮𝘦𝘢𝘯𝘪𝘯𝘨"...that's untrue. EVERY [big] death of a main/important character in GRRMs ASOIAF has had "meaning", it didn't just happen bc "sUbvErt eXpectatIons" of classical fantasy:

• Ned made LOTS of key mistakes that cost him his head. Main one being giving Cersei a warning, a literal heads-up...which cost him HIS head in the end.

• Robb died bc he betrayed a a petty man who lusted for power/identity. He was warned not to cross Walder Frey & he did it & broke his oath & Frey took an opportunity to align himself with a bigger power.

• Jon is bleeding out/dead bc his Brothers feel he's betraying the point/code of the Watch & they already believed him to be a turncloak, etc

• Tywin died bc Tyrion finally hit his limit with his father -- they changed it in the show, but the overarching reasoning for Tyrion killing tywin is mostly the same.

• Joffrey died bc the Tyrells want their own as Queen & Joff is a monster who needed to be put down & Tommen would be "easier" to control/manipulate. Littlefinger also used it as a power move for himself & to get Sansa, etc.

The majority of the deaths in GoT/ASOIAF have "meaning" of why they happened. I dislike when it's dumbed down to saying GRRM is "nihilistic & cynical"...no, characters made choices & all choices have real consequences & some cost some people their life in-world.

BUT I do get what you're saying overall about the State of Fantasy...& GRRM finishing his series 😅

& gods know I agree (for different reasons, im sure) about HOTD -- I do blame the showrunners for this.

My current project of fantasy storytelling uses the notion that, even if your pre-desposed nature would lean you towards a lifestyle of debauchery, hedonism and selfishness, when push comes to shove you can still defy those past sins and rise above them to become a savior, a hero people can rally behind to fight for a better tomorrow. Hopefully, one day people can ready my story and come away feeling that intention. Because that's what I want to read, so why not write it myself?

replies.author: @deangreen2567
replies.text:
It depends on how you view things. As incredible as LOTR is, nostalgia does play a role in our shared reverence for it. Take Game of Thrones as a prime example (off the top of my head.):

- Yoren of the Night’s Watch makes a last stand against the King’s soldiers, out of nothing more than principle. 

- Ser Davos accepting punishment, imprisonment, and shame in the name of turning Stannis away from Melissandre. 

- Jaimie attacks his own man for dishonourably stabbing Ned in the back of his leg during their fight. 

- Qhorin Halfhand gives his life to allow Jon Snow to better infiltrate the wildlings, saving Jon’s own life in the process. 

- A hundred men guard the wall against a hundred thousand. Grenn gives his life to hold the gate. The giant gives his to get through it. Jon and Mance share a drink to their names. 

- When Jon tries to leave the Wall, learning of his father’s beheading, it’s his friends who chase him in to the woods, breaking the rules themselves to follow him. 

- Jon recognising that every Lord Commander before him had ‘fails’ to guard the realms of men by warring against the wildlings. And breaking all custom to try to make amends. 

- Jaimie saving Brienne from being molested when they’re captured. 

- Theon saving Sansa from Ramsay. Willing to die to do so. 

- Jojen and Meera Reed risking their lives to take Bran beyond the wall. Jojen knowing that he’ll die doing so. 

- Lyanna Mormont siding with Jon Snow even though he’s a bastard (in her eyes) and her oath doesn’t necessarily compel her to.  

There are countless examples of honour and valour displayed throughout the show. They’re just not quite so theatrical, and are often done by ‘B’ and ‘C’ tier characters.

author: @TomStrongTDS
text:
That’s exactly why A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms works so well for me. It follows one man’s story, which makes it feel personal and easy to connect with. Game of Thrones only really worked because the casting and acting were strong enough to hold together all the moving parts. House of the Dragon feels more scattered, and without consistently great performances it comes off colder.
With Dunk, you’re actually following someone human. He’s big, kind, and a bit unsure of himself, just trying to do the right thing. The performance feels grounded and honest, which really works and honestly reminds me of Liam Neeson. Because the story stays focused on him, you can identify with him rather than just watch events happen, and that’s what makes it so much more engaging.
